# Simultaneous enzymatic and SERS properties of bifunctional chitosanmodified popcorn-like Au-Ag nanoparticles for high sensitive detection of melamine in milk powder


Junrong Li2 , Guannan Zhang2 , Lihua Wang, Aiguo Shen1 , Jiming Hu

Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biology and Medicine (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan430072, China


# a r t i c l e i n f o


Article history: Received2February2015Received in revised form14March2015Accepted24March2015

Available online1April2015

Keywords: Popcorn-like nanoparticles SERS

Peroxidase-like activity

Melamine


# 1. Introduction


Surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) is a promising spectroscopic technique, which greatly extends the role of standard Raman Spectroscopy. In recent years, SERS spectroscopy has attracted enormous interest in thefield of molecular multiplex detection [1], pathogen and live cell sensing [2], tissues diagnosis and in vivo bioimaging [3]. One of the advantages of SERS spectroscopy is that it can provide sufficient sensitivity for trace analysis. Taking advantage of the SERS signal of4-mercaptobenzoic acid (4-MBA), Chen et al. [4] successfully detected rabbit IgG with the limit of detection of1 -0.1fg/mL. In our group, α -thrombin was sensitively detected with the detection limit of10fM [5]. SERS intensity can be enhanced when the excitation-lasers ' length matches the optical absorption of Raman reporters. This phenomenon is called surface enhanced resonance Raman scattering (SERRS), which is able to make the enhancement factor increase about100times [6]. Furthermore, SERS substrates, acting as Raman signal amplifiers, their size distribution, geometry, chemical composition and surface chemistry produce an effect on the

E-mail address: agshen@whu.edu.cn (A. Shen).

1Fax: þ 86 27 68752136.

2These authors contributed equally to this work.


# a b s t r a c t


In this work, we suggest a chitosan-modified popcorn-like Au -Ag nanoparticles (CSPNPs) based assay for high sensitive detection of melamine, in which CSPNPs not only provide with an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity but also act as surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) substrates. CSPNPs can catalyze the oxidation of3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2to the charge transfer complex (CTC), which contributes to a tremendous surface-enhanced resonant Raman scattering (SERRS) signals with632.8nm laser excitation. The target molecule melamine can generate an additional compound with H2O2, which means the available amount of H2O2for the oxidation of TMB reduced. Correspondingly, the SERRS intensity of CTC is decreased. The decreased Raman intensity is proportional to the concentration of melamine over a wide range from10nM to50 μ M ( R2 ¼ 0.989), with a limit of detection (LOD) of8.51nM. Moreover, the proposed highly selective method is fully capable of rapid, separation-free detection of melamine in milk powder.

& 2015Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Raman enhancement ability. Gold (Au) and silver (Ag) nanoparticles (NPs) are the most commonly used Raman enhancing platforms. Various shapes and sizes of Au and Ag NPs [7 -11] have been synthesized, such as nanospheres, nanoshells, nanorods and multibranched metal NPs. The formation of ' hot spots ' in the tips of multibranched metal NPs increases tremendously the Raman signal [12]. Besides, the increased surface area relative to that of a sphere of equivalent size allows more Raman reporter molecules to be attached on multibranched metal NPs, which give rise to a further increase of Raman signal. Typically, surfactants [7, 9, 13, 14], such as cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), polyethylene glycol (PEG) and poly (N-vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP), were necessary for the formation of multibranched NPs. However, the potential toxicity and difficulty of replacing these surfactants have a negative impact on their further applications.

Au NPs can not only act as excellent SERS substrates, but also have been demonstrated to serve as catalysts for a number of reactions under a range of experimental conditions. It has been demonstrated that when dispersed as ultrafine particles and supported on metal oxides, Au exhibits an extraordinary high activity for low-temperature catalytic reactions [15]. The smaller Au NPs acting as oxidase have been utilized to catalyze glucose oxidation [16]. Ramanavicien [17] and his co-workers demonstrated that Au NPs can act as redox mediator for glucose oxidase in a spectrophotometrical method. Their results indicated that

Fig. 1. (A) SEM image of CSPNPs with the size of80nm. (B) The photograph of different reaction solutions incubated at45 ° C for10min. (C, D) Typical absorption spectra, Raman spectra of different reaction solutions, respectively. (a: H2O2, TMB, CSPNPs, b: TMB, CSPNPs. c: H2O2, CSPNPs, d: H2O2, TMB, e: CSPNPs.)

electrons from GOx via Au NPs were transferred towards2, 6-dichloroindophenol sodiumsalt hydrate (DCPIP) effectively, which was comparable with electron transfer via redox mediator N-methylphenazonium methyl sulfate (PMS) at some conditions. Other researchers have reported that Au NPs showed desirable catalytic ability towards some heterocyclic compounds as well. In our group [18], the synergetic effect between small Au NPs and2,5-dimercapto-1,3,4-thiadiazole (DMcT) -Ag infinite coordination polymers (ICPs) was also proved to be provided with high catalytic activity towards the degradation of methylene blue even without adding cosubstrate. Xiong et al. [19] discovered that the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) was remarkably enhanced in the presence of graphene -Au nanocomposites.

In addition to catalyze the above mentioned chemicals, Au (or Au -Ag alloy) NPs can also serve as peroxidase mimetics to catalyze the oxidation of2,2 ′ -azinobis3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) [20], 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) [21] and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) [22] in the presence of H2O2. Recently, Jv et al. [23] made a surprising discovery that positively-charged Au NPs with the average size of34nm possess an intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. Many peroxidase mimetics, including BSA-stabilized Au clusters [21], CuS NPs [24], water disposable silicon dots [25] and CoFe2O4magnetic NPs [26], have been reported. The enzyme mimetics can overcome the drawbacks of nature enzymes, such as the catalytic ability and stability are susceptible to environmental conditions [27].

Melamine, with a chemical formula of C3H6N4, is commonly used in the synthesis of melamine formaldehyde resins for producing plastics, coatings, adhesives, laminates and commercialfilters [28]. Unfortunately, melamine was once illegally added to dairy products to make a misleading result of high-level total nitrogen content, which can lead to the formation of insoluble crystals in kidneys causing the renal failure and even death. Classical detection methods for melamine, such as mass spectrometry (MS) [29], gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC -MS) [30], liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry (LC -MS) [31], and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [32], inevitably need time-consuming sample pretreatments and sophisticated instruments. Recently, more simple and sensitive methods in thefield of spectroscopy have been created. Some groups detected melamine based on a colorimetric visual inspection of the NPs solution color change upon melamine interaction [33 -36]. Sadly, the selectivity could not be ensured using the bare NPs because the existing interference substances can make a false positive response, while the modified NPs need tedious modification processes. Also, SERS spectroscopy has been explored in the detection of melamine. On one hand, the enhancement of Raman signal of melamine was realized through depositing NPs on solid materials [37 -39], which usually suffered from lack of reproducibility due to the poor homogeneity of molecular distribution on the SERS substrates. On the other hand, SERS analysis of melamine has been demonstrated in liquid. Good result was obtained utilizing Ag NPs as SERS substrates with directly detecting the Raman signal of melamine [40].

On the other hand, a sensitive indirect method to detect melamine in milk via SERS was developed by using Au NPs as SERS substrates [41], but the linearity response of this method limited the practical application in routine assay.

The combination of SERS with peroxidase-catalyzed reaction will open up new opportunities for high sensitive detection of analytes. Recently, Yu et al. [42] successfully detected the human cardiac isoform of troponin T (cTnT) in human serum with a wider linear range and a more sensitive detection limit than traditional chromogenic tests by a method incorporated SERS technique and an enzyme catalysis-based bioassay. Ding et al. [43] detected melamine with Fe3O4magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) acting as peroxidase mimetics, which was built on the reaction between H2O2and melamine. However, Fe3O4MNPs only serve as catalysts and the detection limit is not low enough. In the present work, the chitosan-modified popcorn-like Au -Ag nanoparticles (CSPNPs) can be conveniently synthesized at room temperature without need of seeds and surfactants. The as-prepared CSPNPs possess highly intrinsic peroxidase mimetic catalytic ability for the oxidation of3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2producing charge transfer complex (CTC), which is most likely due to the following facts: the catalytic ability of Au in the NPs, the positively charged property [23] endowed by CS and high surface area of this new nanostructure. The proposed CSPNPs play a dual role of SERS substrates and peroxidase mimetics. Since melamine can generate an additional compound with H2O2, the addition of melamine will lead to the decrease of the SERRS intensity of CTC. Thus, melamine can be determined indirectly. This work provides a simple, sensitive and selective method for the determination of melamine in milk powder.


# 2. Experimental



# 2.1. Chemicals and reagents


HAuCl4 /C1 3H2O was purchased from Sigma. Ascorbic acid, silver nitrate, glucose, lactose, CaCl2 /C1 2H2O, KCl, NH4Cl, ZnCl2, H2O2 (30wt%) and melamine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the above chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. The milk powder was purchased from the local supermarket. Chitosan (CS) was purchased from Aladdin (deacetylated: 95%, viscosity: 100 -200mPa s). 3,3 ′ ,5,5 ′ -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) (BR) and2,2 ′ -azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) (BR) were purchased from Shanghai kayou Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Cysteine (BR), glycine (BR), and lysine (BR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water with an electrical resistance larger than18.2M Ω cm was used throughout the experiment.


# 2.2. Instruments


A UV-2550UV -vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) was used to record the absorption spectra of different reaction solutions. SERRS measurements were performed with a confocal microscope (Jobin -Yvon HR-800). He -Ne laser with632.8nm radiations was used for excitation. Spectra were collected with50long working distance lens. The signal was integrated for10s twice accumulations. Scanning Electron Microscope images were obtained with a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (SIGMA) microscope. Zeta potentials of CSPNPs were measured on a Nano-ZS ZEN3600particle sizer (Malvern Instruments). Concentrations of Au/Ag were determined with an Inductively Coupled Plasma-Atomic Emission Spectrometry (ICP-AES, IRIS Intrepid II XSP, Thermo Electron Corporation). Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and Energy

Dispersive X-Ray Spectroscopy (EDX) measurements for CSPNPs were performed on a JEOL JEM-2010microscope.


# 2.3. Synthesis of CSPNPs


The CSPNPs were conveniently synthesized by ascorbic acid reduction of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate in the presence of chitosan and silver nitrate. Briefly80nm CSPNPs can be prepared as follows: 150 μ L of29mM HAuCl4solution was added to20mL ultrapure water, and then100 μ L10mg/mL of chitosan (dissolved in5% acetic acid) was added under stirring. One minute later, 60 μ L of6mM silver nitrate was injected into the solutions, and80 μ L of0.1M ascorbic acid was added immediately. After the introduction of ascorbic acid, a colorless stage would maintain for about5s, followed by the emergence of blue color within1min indicating the formation of CSPNPs. No obvious color changes could be observed after1min. The mixture was kept stirring for3h. When the volume of CS was changed to200 μ L and40 μ L, 50nm and120nm CSPNPs can be synthesized, respectively. Thefinal products were purified using centrifugation at3500rpm for15min, and then washing and redispersing in ultrapure water. This process was repeated three times.


# 2.4. CSPNPs peroxidase mimetic-based melamine assay


The experiment conditions were as follows: 100 μ L of melamine corresponding to afinal concentration as0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 20, 30, 40and50 μ M, 10 μ L of H2O2 (50mM), 540 μ L of10mM acetate buffer (pH4.0), 150 μ L of TMB (5mM) and200 μ L of CSPNPs were incubated at45 ° C for10min. After that, the mixtures were kept in an ice-water bath for10min to terminate the reaction before Raman measurements. 6 μ L offinal solution was mounted in a capillary tube with0.2mm wall thickness and subsequentlyfixed on the sample stage of a spectrometer for focusing measurements. Finally the standard curve measurements were conducted based on the SERRS peaks of CTC at1191cm /C0 1and the concentrations of melamine.


# 2.5. Kinetic analysis


The reaction kinetics for the catalytic oxidation of TMB was conducted by recording the absorption spectra at652nm within a1min interval using a Shimadzu UV-2550spectrophotometer. The reaction was conducted at45 ° C with200 μ L CSPNPs in540 μ L of10mM NaAc -HAc buffer (pH4) in the presence of varied concentrations of TMB or H2O2. The apparent kinetic parameters were calculated according to the Michaelis -Menten equation:

where ν is the initial velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, Kmax is the Michaelis constant and [ S ] is the concentration of substrate.


# 2.6. Selectivity comparison and recovery test for melamine detection


To illustrate the selectivity of the proposed method for melamine detection, control experiments were conducted. 100 μ L of20 μ M melamine was incubated with10 μ L of H2O2 (50mM), 150 μ L of TMB (5mM) and540 μ L of10mM acetate buffer (pH4.0) at45 ° C for10min. After that, the mixture was kept in a cold-ice bath for10min to terminate the reaction. For comparison, 100 μ L of interfering substances (cystenine, Vitamine C, lysine, NH4Cl200 μ M and glucose, glycine, lactose, KCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2 0.2mM) instead of melamine was separately conducted in a

similar way. Then, 6 μ L offinal solution was mounted in a capillary tube with0.2mm wall thickness and subsequentlyfixed on the sample stage of a spectrometer for focusing measurements.

To test the recovery and practical applicability of the method for real samples, purified milk powder samples were tested in the method. Firstly, different amounts of melamine were added to1.0g of milk powder corresponding to afinal concentration of melamine as10, 20, 30, 40 μ M. Secondly, the milk powder was treated by a simple extraction procedure. Specifically, 1mL of acetonitrile, 1mL of CCl3COOH, and9mL of water were added into1.0g of milk powder. Then the mixed solution was ultrasonically extracted for20min and then centrifugated at2500rpm for10min. 100 μ L of supernatant instead of melamine was conducted in a way similar to the above mentioned about selectivity comparison.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. The synthesis of CSPNPs


The CSPNPs were conveniently synthesized by ascorbic acid reduction of hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) in the presence of chitosan and silver nitrate. Compared with other existing methods for the formation of multibranched NPs, CSPNPs were formed under room temperature with no need for seeds and surfactants. Besides, the as-prepared CSPNPs were environment friendly and biocompatible, because chitosan is a natural and nontoxic polysaccharide. From the SEM image (Fig. 1a), some ' nanodots ' with the size of about10nm, just like the petals of popcorns, can be observed on the surface of CSPNPs. TEM image (Fig. S1) further confirms the result. A hypothesized growth process is organized as follows: fi rstly, primary Au nuclei were formed due to the reduction of HAuCl4. Soon afterwards, silver ions preferentially deposited on Au nuclei and formed a silver layer due to the underpotential deposition (UPD) [44]. Then Au started growing via the concerted action of both reagent reduction and galvanic replacement. Secondly, the unstable Au crystals underwent agglomeration because of the requirement to reduce the overall surface energy [45] and the adhesion of CS. Thirdly, branches produced from the surface of the agglomerates due to anisotropic growth. This is because the growth of Au crystals followed more energetically favorable directions with the depletion of reactants [45]. The size of CSPNPs with a high monodispersity can be adjusted by changing the amount of regents. The composition of80nm CSPNPs with the original Ag/Au mole ratio of0.08:1was investigated by ICP-AES, EDX (Fig. S2). To conduct ICP-AES detection, CSPNPs were isolated by centrifugation at3500rpm in15min, washed three times and subsequently dissolved in aqua regia. ICPAES data shows an Ag/Au mole ratio of0.07:1, which indicates the exact amount of Au/Ag in the solution. EDX result shows an Ag/Au mole ratio of0.06:1. These results are consistent with the hypothesis. Due to the fact that CS possesses the positively charged -NH3 þ group under acidic conditions, zeta potential of the CSPNPs (80nm) was positive ( þ 38.47mV).


# 3.2. Verification of the peroxidise-like activity of CSPNPs and sensing mechanism


To assess whether CSPNPs would exhibit peroxidase mimetic catalytic ability, we tested catalytic oxidation peroxidase substrates TMB and ABTS in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 1B and Fig. S3, TMB and ABTS can be separately oxidized into a blue and green product in the presence of H2O2and CSPNPs. In contrast, the mixture of other substances did not show characteristic color or only lightly. Furthermore, UV -vis spectroscopy was used to characterize the different solutions. Fig. 1C shows that CSPNPs, TMB and their mixture did not have absorptions at370nm and652nm. Compared with the absorption spectrum of the mixture of H2O2and TMB, strong absorptions at370nm and652nm were observed in the presence of CSPNPs in the H2O2and TMB solution, which demonstrates that the reaction of H2O2with TMB was greatly accelerated by CSPNPs. Besides, to prove the better catalytic and Raman signal enhancement ability of CSPNPs, different materials were compared, including sodium citrate modified popcorn-like NPs (Na3Ct-CSPNPs), chitosan (CS), sodium citrate modified30nm Au NPs (Na3Ct-AuNPs) and chitosan modified30nm AuNPs (CS-AuNPs). Two conclusions can be drawn from Fig. S4A, 4B, 4C. Firstly, positive-charged NPs (CSPNPs, CS-AuNPs) encompass a better catalytic ability than negative-charged NPs (Na3Ct-CSPNPs, Na3Ct-AuNPs), which consists with the former report [23]. Secondly, CSPNPs, though the total size is bigger than CS-AuNPs, show better results both catalytic and signal enhancement ability. The above mentioned facts can be explained in the following reasons. Au in the NPs greatly facilitated the catalytic reaction, which is line with the reports that Au can catalyze TMB [21], ABTS [20], OPD [22] by H2O2and Au can act as redox mediator [17]. Besides, the positive charged property endowed by CS would influence the absorption of H2O2and the particle-mediated electron transfer processes [23], which increased the catalytic ability of the NPs. Furthermore, the increased surface area owing to the appearance of ' nanodots ' enhanced the catalytic ability.

The oxidation of TMB by H2O2went through a two-step twoelectron reaction (Fig. S5) [46]. The blue product belonging to charge transfer complex (CTC) [46] is attributed to the reversible complexation of the diamine andfinal diimine. According to a recent report, this CTC product can be analyzed by resonance Raman scattering using a laser excitation of632.8nm. In our present work, the Raman signal intensity was tremendously decreased by removing CSPNPs from the solutions in the method of centrifugation (Fig. S6), which indicated that CSPNPs act as not only catalysts but also SERS substrates. We further characterized the peroxidase-like activity of CSPNPs through Raman Spectroscopy (Fig. 1D). Raman bands assigned to CTC are summarized into Table S1. The analyses yielded consistent results as UV -vi s did. All of the results demonstrated that the as-prepared CSPNPs did possess peroxidase mimetic catalytic ability.

The basic design of the strategy is demonstrated as Scheme1. In this system, CSPNPs can catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2producing CTC, and H2O2will react with melamine generating an addition compound [43]. Thus, the SERRS signal of CTC will be reduced once melamine is added. The amounts of melamine can be determined according to the decrease of SERRS intensity of CTC. The enhanced Raman band at1191cm /C0 1is selected as

Scheme1. Strategy illustration of the SERRS detection of melamine using CSPNPs.

Fig. 2. Optimization of experimental conditions. (A) The pH of the reaction buffer, (B) the incubation temperature, (C) the enough usage amounts of CSPNPs, (D) the amounts of TMB, (E) the concentration of H2O2, and (F) the particle size of CSPNPs.

quantitative standards for it gives the lowest detection limit of the three enhanced Raman bands. This work provides a simple, sensitive and selective method for melamine determination in milk powder.


# 3.3. Optimization of the reaction conditions


The catalytic ability of CSPNPs depends on pH, temperature, TMB and H2O2concentrations and the size of CSPNPs. We measured the peroxidase-like activity of CSPNPs while varying pH from2to11, the temperature from25 ° C to65 ° C. The optimal pH and temperature were approximately pH4and45 ° C (Fig. 2A, B), which were very similar to the values for horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [27]. The influence of TMB on the reaction is shown in Fig. 2D. No significant change can be observed after150 μ L, thus the best volume of TMB was150 μ L. The concentration of H2O2was critical to our experiment. To ensure sensitivity, the concentration of H2O2

was optimized following two steps. Firstly, certain amounts of melamine were added to a series of mixtures of H2O2, TMB while varying H2O2concentration from10mM to100mM. For comparison, control experiments were conducted similarly without adding melamine. Those, with the same concentration of H2O2while exhibit color differences, were chosen as candidates to conduct Raman detection. Secondly, thefinal optimal value was established by the sensitivity of Raman (Fig. 2E). The value of y shows that the differences of SERRS intensity of CTC at1191cm /C0 1between experiments with the same concentration of H2O2divided the

amounts of melamine. Apparently, 50mM should be chosen as the optimal H2O2concentration. The effect of the particle size of CSPNPs on our experiments is shown in Fig. 2F, the SERRS intensity of CTC at1191cm /C0 1was the strongest when the size of CSPNPs was about80nm, which was a concerted action of catalysis and SERS enhancement. In addition, to ensure the reaction can be completed within the shortest time, the best amounts of CSPNPs were200 μ L (Fig. 2C). The reaction time was also observed. As shown in Fig. S7, the Raman signal intensity at1191cm /C0 1gradually increased from2min to10min, but no significant change was observed after10min. Therefore, we consider that the reaction can be terminated at10min.


# 3.4. Influence of H2O2and TMB concentrations on reaction rate


To investigate the catalytic mechanism of CSPNPs, the apparent steady-state kinetic parameters were determined by changing the concentrations of TMB and H2O2, respectively. As shown in Fig. S8A and B, the reaction rate showed hyperbolic dependence on the concentration of substrates (H2O2and TMB). This was in agreement with Michaelis -Menten kinetics. The kinetic parameters maximum initial velocity ( Vmax) and Michaelis -Menten

The error bars illustrate the standard deviations of three independent measurements.

constant ( Km) are correspondingly ' a ' and ' b ' parameters of hyperbolic function y ¼ ax /( b þ x ) [47, 48]. The results are summarized in Table1. For enzymes, Km demonstrates the affinity for the substrate. So, a smaller Km means a higher affinity and a stronger catalytic capability. The apparent Km value of CSPNPs with H2O2as the substrate was higher than HRP. However, the Km value of CSPNPs was2times lower than Fe3O4MNPs [27], which has been used as peroxidase enzyme to detect glucose [49] and melamine [43] with good performance. Thus, it is reasonable to believe that CSPNPs will perform better in terms of melamine detection. The apparent Km value of CSPNPs with TMB as the substrate was higher than HRP and Fe3O4MNPs [27], meaning that CSPNPs have a lower affinity for TMB.


# 3.5. Analysis of melamine with high sensitivity and selectivity


Under the optimized conditions above, qualitative and sensitive detection of melamine was achieved by monitoring the SERRS peaks of CTC at1191cm /C0 1 . As shown in Fig. 3A, the color gradually became lighter with the increasing amounts of melamine. As a result, the SERRS intensity of CTC at1191cm /C0 1also decreased gradually, which can be seen from Fig. 3B. A linear relationship (Fig. 3c) was obtained between the SERRS intensity of CTC at1191cm /C0 1and the melamine concentration from10nM to50 μ M ( R2 ¼ 0.989) with a detection limit of8.51nM (LOD ¼ 3.3SD/S, where SD is the standard deviation of the response well-known from statistics and S is the slope of the calibration curve in the initial stages). This detection limit is far lower than the safety limits of melamine ingestion (20 μ M in the US and EU; 8 μ M in infant formula in China). Compared with the detection using Fe3O4MNPs [43], the proposed method provides a much lower detection limit and a broader linear range. Also, the detection limit obtained in our work was better than the results reported by using the SERS [39, 50] and colorimetric methods [35, 36].

Considering that the existence of some potential interfering substances, such as glucose, lactose, Vitamin C, common ions and some nitrogen containing organic molecules, might affect the detection of melamine in real samples analysis, the selectivity of this method was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, compared with other species, only melamine induces a dramatic decrease of Raman signal intensity. These results indicate that the proposed method is amenable to selectively detect melamine.


# 3.6. Application in real samples


To further confirm the reliability of the proposed method in practical applications, the detection of melamine in milk powder was performed. Certain amounts of melamine were spiked in the samples, after which the milk powder was treated by a simple extraction procedure. The results are summarized in Table2. The recoveries of the real samples are in the range90.86 -106.29% with the relative standard deviation of1.66 -4.29%. Two other milk powder samples were treated in the similar way, and the results are summarized in Table S2. The desirable results obviously demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed method for the determination of melamine in milk powder samples.


# 4. Conclusions


In summary, CSPNPs were conveniently synthesized which possessed a higher intrinsic peroxidase-like activity. Furthermore, trace melamine was sensitively detected based on the peroxidase mimetic ability of CSPNPs and reaction of melamine with H2O2. In our system, CSPNPs not only acted as catalysts but also served as enhancement substrates. To the best of our knowledge, this is thefirst time that melamine could be detected by combining peroxidase-catalyzed reaction with SERRS. We expect ourfindings will be utilized to a wide range of new potential applications including biotechnology, environmental chemistry and medicine.


# Appendix A. Supplementary material


Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2015.03.050.
